Title: Navigating the rise of finfluencers: a multidimensional quality framework for strategic partnerships
URL: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1057/s41264-025-00334-7
Time Published: 2026-03-04T16:15:37Z
Full Content:
In the film Wall Street: Money Never Sleeps (2010), the antihero Gordon Gekko reflects on the moral ambiguity of modern financial services, stating: “(.) the beauty of the deal [taking other people’s money]: no one is responsible”. This phrase is gaining new relevance as financial influencers (finfluencers) are increasingly filling the financial advice gap by operating on various social platforms within a legal gray zone and with limited accountability (Barrett 2022; Moss et al. 2022; Pflücke 2022, 2023). Even though there is no common definition yet, we follow Mölders et al. (2025), who define finfluencers as “personal brands who build relationships with an engaged audience through a regular flow of consistent, authentic and distinctive content posted on at least one social media platform that relates to, but is not limited to, personal finance and investment tips.”Footnote 1 While finfluencers can be seen as a distinct subset of expert-oriented and utilitarian influencers, their approaches diverge significantly: some position themselves as expert voices, drawing on formal qualifications, licenses, or professional experience to establish authority, while others adopt a utilitarian role, providing simplified, accessible financial tips for lay audiences (Mölders et al. 2025).Footnote 2 This dual positioning highlights the tension between perceived expertise and actual credentials, as well as the differing levels of accountability tied to each role. Comparable distinctions have been noted in the wider influencer marketing literature (e.g., Campbell and Farrell 2020; Vrontis et al. 2021; Han and Balabanis 2024) and are similarly relevant for other regulated domains, including medicine and politics (e.g., Ding et al. 2023). The rise of finfluencers was fueled by a number of factors, including the continuous expansion of global internet and social media usage (We Are Social et al. 2025), the pandemic (Boyde 2023; Davies 2023; Fava 2023), the growing valuation of global influencer marketing exceeding $30 billion in 2025 (Statista 2025), and the rising demand for financial literacy and advice (Calcagno and Monticone 2015; Barrett 2022). While the investment volume driven by finfluencers is difficult to pinpoint, finfluencers are currently outperforming their peers in other influencer niches, with double the follower growth on platforms like TikTok and Instagram (Emplifi 2024). Indeed, they seem to have a significant impact on the financial decision-making of younger generations as recent surveys show: e.g., in the United Kingdom (UK), over 44% of Gen Z state using information from social media for investment decisions; in the United States (US), 37% of Gen Z say finfluencers play a major role in their investment decisions (FINRA Foundation and CFA Institute 2023); and in Germany, more than half of Gen Z and Millennials view social media as an alternative to traditional financial advice (German Federal Financial Supervisory Authority [BaFin] 2024). However, finfluencers tend to concentrate on high-risk asset classes such as cryptocurrencies, where social media hype and fear of missing out (FOMO) often drive investment decisions (Yamamoto et al. 2020; FINRA Foundation and CFA Institute 2023; Merkley et al. 2024). A similar phenomenon has been observed in NFTs (non-fungible tokens), where pricing and trading volumes are closely tied to finfluencer activity rather than fundamentals (e.g., Kapoor et al. 2022; Horky et al. 2023). Thus, finfluencers can also be seen as facilitators of speculative retail investing (Mölders et al. 2025) and the crypto ecosystem. This is consistent with headlines such as “Eight Twitter finance gurus charged with criminal pump-and-dump scheme” (Robertsen 2022), which highlight the financial risks associated with “finfluencing charlatans” (adapted from Berk and Binsbergen 2022). In this prominent case, also known as Atlas Trading, eight Twitter-based finfluencers were indicted by US authorities for stock manipulation and defrauding retail investors of over $100 million (Securities and Exchange Commission [SEC] 2022b; US Department of Justice 2022). Beyond this, there have been numerous cases of fraudulent promotion of financial services (also by celebrities) and “get rich quick” investment schemes via social media (e.g., Lukpat 2022; Michaels 2022; Davies 2023). In response to such significant financial losses and consumer harm, regulators and authorities around the globe are turning their eyes on finfluencers (e.g., Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets [AFM] 2021a); Australian Securities and Investments Commission 2022; BaFin 2022; SEC 2022a, 2022b; European Securities and Markets Authority 2023; Financial Conduct Authority 2023a, 2023b, 2023c; Securities and Exchange Board of India 2023). For instance, the German regulatory body has issued the following warning: “On social media, you will find real experts – but many self-proclaimed experts as well. Both types are also to be found among the financial influencers (known as ‘finfluencers’)” (BaFin 2022). It is therefore not surprising that current research predominately discusses consumer harm and protection and corresponding legal perspectives (e.g., Miller 2022; Pflücke 2022, 2023; Hall 2023; Khurana 2023; Ramaswamy 2023; Stefanou 2023; Guan 2023, 2024). Apart from the consumer protection perspective, finfluencers could also be integrated as strategic business partners (Enke and Borchers 2019; Borchers and Enke 2021). This is especially relevant for financial services marketing (e.g., Mensah and Amenuvor 2022; Ho and Amin 2023; Nguyen et al. 2024). However, businesses seeking strategic partnerships with finfluencers are at risk of damaging their own reputation by potentially collaborating with finfluencing charlatans (e.g., Campbell and Farrell 2020; McMullan 2023). In consequence, we perceive a gap in the current academic discourse regarding the quality of finfluencers, which has been addressed only one-dimensionally based on their investing performance (Kakhbod et al. 2023; Espeute and Preece 2024). In response, we address this gap and follow calls for further research on new technologies, especially social media and finfluencers (Bhaskar et al. 2023; Hudders and Lou 2023; Bapat and Vashisht 2024; Yeo et al. 2024) and provide a complementary solution to economically costly over-regulation (Berk and Binsbergen 2022). Thus, our study aims to derive a multidimensional finfluencer quality (FinQ) framework to guide finfluencers’ stakeholders to select high-quality strategic business partners. Hence, we formulate our guiding research question (RQ) as follows: RQ What are quality indicators of trustworthy finfluencers and how can these indicators be systematically integrated into a multidimensional quality framework for facilitating strategic partnerships? We applied a mixed-method approach to answer the RQ. In the first step, we conceptualized a preliminary FinQ framework based on existing theory. In a second step, we used 12 expert interviews to refine and complement the preliminary FinQ framework, which resulted in the identification of 40 quality indicators (N = 40). These quality indicators can be categorized into five quality dimensions (QDs): (QD1) Compliance, (QD2) Content and Communication, (QD3) Credibility, (QD4) Profile Structure and (QD5) Engagement. In a last step, the refined FinQ framework was validated in terms of practical applicability and operationalization by a subsequent multiple-case study selecting five German-speaking finfluencers on Instagram. While our study tackles quality uncertainty surrounding finfluencers, it also encourages businesses to form strategic partnerships with credible, high-quality finfluencers, particularly in financial services marketing. The FinQ framework supports the professionalization of such partnerships by enabling more informed selection decisions. While it cannot fully eliminate charlatans, it helps financial service providers identify trustworthy partners, avoid reputational risks, and raise standards over time. Beyond financial services, the framework can also inform regulatory efforts, consumer protection, and strategic collaborations in other areas, such as listed firms’ financial communication or governmental financial literacy campaigns. The business model of finfluencers involves referral and reward networks potentially leading to conflicts of interest (Campbell and Farrell 2020; Reingen and Kernan 1986; Verlegh et al. 2013). Such networks usually compensate finfluencers on a commission basis upon referral of a client for a specific financial service or product (e.g., by using affiliate links or promo codes) (AFM 2021b). Thus, we assume that finfluencers face moral hazard because of their conflicted business model (Arrow 1985; Alon et al. 2021). In this context, when the lemon principle, referring to the problem of quality uncertainty in markets with information asymmetry between buyers and sellers (Akerlof 1970), is applied to finfluencers (sellers), their stakeholders (buyers; here consumers or followers and businesses seeking partnerships) face quality uncertainty, wherein we assume finfluencers are better informed. As a solution, Leland (1979) found that minimum quality constraints or licensing requirements tackle quality uncertainty in high-skill professions (e.g., lawyers or doctors). This can be applied to finfluencers, where typically rather uninformed followers consume conflicted financial (unregulated) advice. However, regulation of finfluencers may also reduce competition in the financial advisory market, which is why regulating finfluencers is not always the best option for consumer welfare depending on the level of consumer harm (Berk and Binsbergen 2022). However, there is currently no literature that empirically assesses the regulation of finfluencers from an economic perspective. While moral hazard provides a useful lens to study commission-driven finfluencer activity, not all creators of financial content on social media operate primarily for personal profit. Many also share content to educate, foster communities, build their personal brand, or even for entertainment (Guan 2023; Mölders et al. 2025). This heterogeneity of finfluencers tempers the reach of the moral hazard argument but still reinforces the need for a quality framework that applies across diverse motivation profiles. FOMO has been identified as a key driver of financial decision-making of Gen Z (FINRA Foundation and CFA Institute 2023), which puts emphasis on how moral hazard stems not only from conflicted incentives on the supply side but also from emotional manipulation on the demand or consumer side. While FOMO is conceptualized as a utility-based reaction to perceived opportunity loss from an economic perspective (Kaddouhah 2024), it can distort rational financial decision-making. Recent empirical studies show that such appeals amplify impulsive, risky and short-term oriented financial decision-making (Friederich et al. 2024; Meyer et al. 2024; Symbiosis and Gandhi 2024; Mölders et al. 2025) and reinforce herding dynamics and overreliance on social heuristics leading to systematic underperformance (Kakhbod et al. 2023; Gerritsen and Regt 2025; Haase et al. 2025). These findings highlight the need to address not only information asymmetries on the supply side, but also the risk of behavioral biases on the consumer side when assessing FinQ. Ultimately, we aim to conceptualize a FinQ framework that helps (corporate) stakeholders navigate the largely unregulated space of financial advice delivered by finfluencers on social media platforms. In markets with information asymmetries, where the true quality of a provider is difficult to assess, such frameworks can reduce uncertainty and support informed decision-making (Stiglitz 2002). Building on this idea, a FinQ framework enabling benchmarking (Cook and Luo 2003; Aiken et al. 2004; Hörner and Lambert 2021) may not only incentivize finfluencers to meet defined quality standards but also foster transparency and comparability. As a result, it might also encourage healthy competition and introduce a form of peer pressure and accountability within the finfluencer market (Calvó-Armengol and Jackson 2010). Quality is described by the international quality management standard ISO 9000:2015 as the “degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of (…) [a service or product] fulfils requirements.” Following this customer-oriented definition, we need to identify quality requirements (or standards) to establish a quality framework for finfluencers. The role of such a “standard setter” can be taken on by different stakeholders including researchers (Soni Raleigh and Foot 2010). In a first step, to measure quality, we must define a set of quality indicators. Therefore, we employ the concept of key performance indicators (KPIs).Footnote 3 Therefore, measuring quality depends on meeting defined KPIs as focal units of the framework.Footnote 4 In a second step, such defined KPIs will be aggregated into quality dimensions (QDs), which will serve as the conceptual cornerstones of the preliminary FinQ framework. This approach is consistent with the grouping of performance metrics into strategic dimensions, as seen in frameworks such as the Balanced Scorecard according to Kaplan and Norton (1992). In addition to considering moral hazard and behavioral biases, FinQ assessment needs to account for an environment of regulatory ambiguity, where jurisdictional inconsistencies and limited enforcement capacity can enable non-compliant or even unlawful practices and emerging forms of financial misconduct (Aggarwal et al. 2022; Miller 2022; Pflücke 2022, 2023; Stefanou 2023; International Organization of Securities Commissions 2025). We acknowledge that compliance under conditions of regulatory ambiguity often operates in a gray zone shaped by evolving standards, limited enforcement, and a strong reliance on self-regulation. These broader structural challenges serve as contextual boundary conditions that reinforce the urgency of establishing transparent and stakeholder-oriented quality criteria. Against this backdrop, we introduce five QDs grounded in the literature that serve as guiding pillars for structuring the FinQ framework: First, compliance with regulation is an inherent prerequisite for quality and gatekeeping dimension for influencer selection, especially in the light of corporate strategic partnerships with finfluencers (Espeute and Preece 2024; Mölders et al. 2025). However, the extent of compliance is dependent on the context, with requirements varying according to the country and the applicable regulation, which often remain fragmented and evolving (Aggarwal et al. 2022; Pflücke 2023). In the development of the FinQ framework, we based our approach on the German and European regulatory framework (see Appendix A). However, such regulatory and legal framework as a basis for compliance requirements must be subject to review and adaptation in each country or market. Consequently, we established QD1 Compliance. Second, we observe that most qualitative KPIs tend to be holistically defined in the existing literature, which is possibly due to their rather limited accessibility and measurability. Thus, we were able to identify rather holistic KPIs such as “post quality” (content quality) and “professional communication” from existing literature (Gräve and Greff 2018; Gräve 2019). However, such KPIs need to be further broken down and refined to be measured accurately. Other connected more specific KPIs refer for example to “playfulness”, which refers to the level of entertainment (Kim and Baek 2022). In response to such holistic content-related and communicative KPIs we formulate QD2 Content and Communication. Third, we also find that “experience” (but also track record) and “expertise” is often associated with influencer quality (e.g., Gräve and Greff 2018; Kim and Baek 2022). This is complemented by KPIs such as “trustworthiness”, “authenticity” or even “attractiveness” of influencers (e.g., Chekima et al. 2020; Primasiwi et al. 2021; Wiedmann and Mettenheim 2021). We refer to attractiveness on a personal level, which has been shown to enhance perceived trustworthiness and expertise through halo effects (Ohanian 1990; Reingen and Kernan 1993). In this context, credibility emerges as a central concept in the literature (e.g., Belanche et al. 2021; Bi and Zhang 2023; Dhun and Dangi 2023). To address the perceived importance of the credibility of finfluencers, we accordingly specify the principal dimension QD3 Credibility. Lastly, influencer marketing practitioners mostly rely on well-established and easily accessible quantitative KPIs such as community size, engagement rate, target group fit, posting frequency, sentiment or impressions (Gräve and Greff 2018; Gräve 2019; Primasiwi et al. 2021). This is also consistent with major influencer audit or due diligence platforms such as HypeAuditor and Upfluence (Alain 2022; Komok 2024). Thus, we derive QD4 Profile Structure and QD5 Engagement. While QD4 assesses how effectively a finfluencer manages their content and cross-platform presence, QD5 focuses on quantitative engagement metrics such as community size and interactions. However, studies by Goldenberg et al. (2024) and Hinz et al. (2011) emphasize that reach alone is insufficient, as true influence depends on content–audience fit, credibility, and network dynamics. This is supported by Lanz et al. (2019) who show that mid-tier influencers can outperform macro-influencers in engagement and trust.Footnote 5 Existing influencer quality frameworks in marketing provide useful foundations for evaluating influencer effectiveness. Leung et al. (2022) highlight influencer selection as a key step, focusing on traits like network size, engagement, authenticity, and brand fit (Gräve and Greff 2018; Lou and Yuan 2019; see also Audrezet et al. 2020). While the FinQ framework reflects general influencer criteria via QD4–5, existing frameworks often overlook finance-specific factors, especially compliance, regulatory risk, and the pitfalls of popularity-driven metrics. FinQ addresses this gap by embedding domain-specific requirements, aligning quality assessment with the unique expectations of financial services. It is built on five core dimensions and over 15 KPIs, which are refined and validated in the following steps (see Table 1; Fig. 1). Conceptual framework Our research is intended to further refine and validate the conceptualized preliminary FinQ framework derived from existing literature. In response to the novelty of the finfluencer phenomenon, we follow an exploratory sequential mixed-method approach according to Creswell and Creswell (2023) for the framework development (see Fig. 2). Therefore, we refined the preliminary FinQ framework through in-depth qualitative analysis of expert interviews, followed by a validation for practical applicability and robustness through a multiple-case study following Dehghani et al. (2022). Research framework Therefore, in the first step, we discussed the preliminary FinQ framework with an expert panel to identify the necessary expertise, partially following the approach proposed by Negrin et al. (2022) and the guidance on qualitative interviewing of Edwards and Holland (2015). Accordingly, an interview guideline (see Appendix B) was derived, which was followed by subsequent pilot interviews (Majid et al. 2017). After a feedback loop, further participants were recruited through online research and direct referrals (Noy 2008). The recruitment process was continued until the point at which theoretical saturation was increasingly evident, as indicated by the level of relevance in Table 2 and the corresponding reduced number of new code categories (quality indicators or KPIs) added sequentially by new interview participants as shown in Table 4 (Hennink et al. 2017). We interviewed various participants, including but not limited to financial journalists, financial service providers, consumer advocates, and finfluencers, capturing multiple perspectives on FinQ to ensure stakeholder triangulation (see column “Stakeholder type” in Table 2; Campbell et al. 2020). The semi-structured expert interviews (59.5 min on average; 12 h in total) were conducted from November 2023 to January 2024. All interviews were held online and recorded using Zoom after verbal and tool-based consent. After recording, the interviews were transcribed using Sonix.ai and subsequently coded and analyzed using the qualitative data analysis software MAXQDA. While fundamentally following the grounded theory of Corbin and Strauss (2015), the focus of this research was to expand the identified quality indicators (KPIs) and refine the preliminary quality framework. In this context, we applied a hybrid deductive–inductive coding approach. The five QDs, derived from literature, were used as top-level thematic categories, while the specific KPIs emerged inductively from the data. Interview transcripts were thematically coded in MAXQDA, with potential quality indicators grouped under the corresponding QD through iterative refinement. Coding was led by one researcher and cross-checked by a second to ensure consistency and interpretive reliability. To further enhance robustness, the built-in generative AI of MAXQDA was used to support the refinement process (Morgan 2023; Zhang et al. 2023). In the subsequent validation, a multiple-case study of individual Instagram accounts (Vaus 2001; Yin 2018) tested the practical applicability of the conceptually derived FinQ framework.Footnote 6 A market benchmark from Zülch et al. (2024) was used to select and benchmark five German-speaking finfluencers with data as of January 2024 (refer to Table 3). The cases were selected based on their market position and popularity, entailing a filtering process (with a minimum of 1,000 followers) and track record (operating on Instagram for at least one year), following the guidance of Miles and Huberman (1994). To ensure variation, cases were selected by gender and topical focus to capture diverse finfluencer types and content strategies. Ten posts per candidate (including pinned and random) were independently assessed in a double-blind review, with discrepancies averaged after a pilot for pretesting and calibration. All expert interviews were conducted in German, and translated quotes are provided throughout the analysis to illustrate key findings (the original German quotations are available upon request). The refinement of the preliminary FinQ framework resulted in a comprehensive set of 40 KPIs (N = 40) within the five previously established QDs. Notably, the most frequently coded KPIs are: (QD1) advertising disclosure and licenses/permission, (QD2) accuracy/correctness, (QD3) expertise/qualification, independence/neutrality and reliability/social proof, (QD4) target group fit and (QD5) community size and reach. In the following, we will use chosen citations of the expert interviews to not only dive deeper into the qualitative data set, but also to support the refined FinQ framework (see Table 4). QD1 Compliance is seen as “(…) the highest priority, it is always the first starting point (…).” (Interviewee 2). Thus, adherence to “Compliance is (…) conditio sine qua non” (Interviewee 4) making it a fundamentally non-negotiable QD with a total of eight KPIs. Consequently, regarding strategic partnerships in the financial services industry, this means managers and practitioners should have “a basic fear, which is reputation at risk” (Interviewee 4). It should be ensured that there is no promotional collaboration with finfluencers who do “not [ensure] one hundred per cent compliance” (Interviewee 4). In response, Compliance therefore follows the “all-or-nothing principle” as gatekeeping dimension. Furthermore, “transparent disclosure of funding” (Interviewee 6) is relevant to avoid conflicts of interest and signal potential moral hazard to followers. In this context, transparency is a high priority regarding advertising disclosure: “if you get money from someone, make it transparent” (Interviewee 7). QD2 Content and Communication encompasses nine exclusively qualitative KPIs, which tends to be a more subjective QD. However, the experts consider “criteria such as variety, ease of understanding and perhaps entertainment value” (Interviewee 3) to be particularly relevant. On the one hand, this dimension primarily concentrates on “the quality of the content, i.e. how high is the quality of the content produced?” (Interviewee 8). On the other hand, the focus is also on “professionalism in communication, (…) [i.e.] how do I communicate with followers, (…) even also if I present myself elsewhere?” (Interviewee 5). Managers and practitioners should therefore pay particular attention to the professionalism of finfluencers when engaging in strategic partnerships. Finally, objectivity is highlighted as an extremely important quality indicator within this QD, even though the business model seems to be conflicted: “One of the key factors is neutrality, incorruptibility [of finfluencers]” (Interviewee 10). QD3 Credibility is seen as another critical QD: “But credibility is, I think, (…) the most important thing.” (Interviewee 10). Following the breakdown and refinement of the KPIs identified in the literature review, a total of nine KPIs were identified for this QD. The experts consider “the personal experience (..) [finfluencers] have [as] very, very important.” (Interviewee 2). In addition, “relevant prior education or self-taught through a track record” (Interviewee 12) are relevant for the experts to assess the credibility of finfluencers. In consequence, “the track record (…) and the (…) expertise [play] a very important role” (Interviewee 8). These KPIs should also be considered by managers and practitioners when screening finfluencers to engage in strategic partnerships. Notably, the experts also stress that “channels where you don’t have a face” (Interviewee 11) or channels “that are based (…) [in a third country] and hide behind masks or pictures” (Interviewee 5) are early warning signs not to engage in strategic partnerships because of reputational risks of potential charlatans. QD4–5 Profile Structure and Engagement mostly cover well-established quantitative KPIs (14 in total), where especially community size and engagement rate are seen as particularly relevant for strategic partnerships: But “We don’t just want to have (…) influencers who have a high reach, we also need good followers who have a high engagement rate” (Interviewee 12). However, some experts hinted not to overestimate QD4 and QD5: (1) as they mostly cover generally applicable influencer KPIs not specific to finfluencers, (2) popular finfluencers “take it all”, (3) dilution of relevance by bots and fake engagement, and (4) there is some overlapping and correlation with the other mostly qualitative QDs (as kind of “residual KPIs” as a consequence of QD1-3). In this context, for a large-scale analysis of high engagement rate outliers and their potential dilution through scams and fakes, especially on Instagram see, e.g., Narassiguin and Sargent (2019). Furthermore, for potential overlapping and correlation of quantitative “traditional” influencer KPIs, see Gräve (2019). We developed a FinQ scorecard based on the refined framework (see Appendix C), revealing diverse quality profiles and content strategies. While some finfluencers combined financial expertise with practical advice, others focused on lifestyle or niche audiences. One candidate was excluded for non-compliance, and another was lacking finance-relevant qualifications. Engagement rates ranged from 0.22% to 1.70%, placing our sample between the 3rd and 40th benchmark percentile, reflecting relatively low levels. This supports prior findings that larger audiences often correlate with weaker engagement, while micro and nano influencers tend to show stronger relative engagement (e.g., Kay et al. 2020; Conde and Casais 2023). Further, the validation demonstrates that the FinQ framework facilitates a differentiation between high-, medium-, and low-quality finfluencers, even though a larger-scale application would be required to establish a robust market benchmark. While individual dimensions (e.g., engagement only) alone provide limited insight, multidimensional integration of qualitative and quantitative indicators reveal distinct quality profiles. Therefore, high-quality finfluencers need to perform consistently well across all QDs, demonstrating strong compliance, expertise and credible communication, while lower-quality finfluencers tend to exhibit selective strengths (e.g. high engagement but weak credibility). This demonstrates the added value of the FinQ framework when it comes to distinguishing reliable strategic partners from those who are less credible, or even potential charlatans. In total, 28 of the original 40 KPIs from the refined FinQ framework were validated and operationalized for the scorecard used in the multiple-case study (see Table 4). To ensure empirical applicability, the selection emphasized practicability and measurability over exhaustive coverage. Specifically, (1) KPIs that were observable and assessable via publicly available social media content (e.g., posting frequency, platform consistency) were prioritized; and (2) KPIs that proved overly subjective, ambiguous, or infeasible for reliable cross-case assessment (e.g., sentiment, self-monitoring, or internal compliance structures) were excluded. Importantly, the removal of 12 KPIs does not imply diminished theoretical relevance; these indicators remain part of the broader conceptual framework but were excluded from scoring due to methodological constraints. A full list of excluded KPIs is provided in Appendix D. To accommodate the varied nature of the KPIs, the FinQ scorecard applies different scale types: binary checks for compliance thresholds, dichotomous ratings for “all-or-nothing” factors like formal qualification, Likert-type ratings for nuanced judgments by analysts, and benchmark-based scoring for quantitative metrics (e.g., engagement rates). This mix ensures rigor while allowing consistent cross-case comparison. We also acknowledge that engagement metrics (QD5) may be distorted by inorganic activity, such as bots or fake accounts, which are known to inflate financial discourse on platforms like Instagram (Narassiguin and Sargent 2019; Kraaijeveld and Smedt 2020). While our scorecard relied on publicly observable engagement data, future applications of the FinQ framework should incorporate bot audit tools and fraud detection to improve the robustness of engagement scoring. The validation demonstrates the FinQ framework’s practical utility and benchmarking potential. It allows companies to identify strategic partners offering a replicable and scalable foundation for finfluencer due diligence in financial services. To enhance practical applicability, we translated the validated dimensions into a concise due diligence checklist (Appendix E). This tool complements the scorecard by offering clear, actionable questions that support consistent, real-world assessments of finfluencers for managerial decision-making. We establish a multidimensional FinQ framework that extends current literature on finfluencers beyond calling for regulation (e.g., Pflücke 2022, 2023). While moral hazard and quality uncertainty persist (AFM 2021b; Arrow 1985; BaFin 2022), conflicted financial advice may still be preferable to none (Chalmers and Reuter 2020; Mölders et al. 2025). Accordingly, the FinQ framework offers a complementary approach to avoid costly over-regulation (Berk and Binsbergen 2022) by enabling stakeholders to distinguish trustworthy finfluencers from charlatans and promote informed consumer decision-making (Stiglitz 2002; Hörner and Lambert 2021). This approach can be reinforced through trust marks, peer pressure, and reward mechanisms that foster professionalization within the finfluencer domain (Cook and Luo 2003; Aiken et al. 2004; Calvó-Armengol and Jackson 2010). At the same time, social media platforms increasingly act as gatekeepers through algorithmic moderation and compliance enforcement (e.g., Schroeder 2025). Their incentive to tackle misleading financial content aligns with broader accountability and regulatory goals, and the FinQ framework could further inform automated “charlatan detection” or other platform-based screening approaches by outlining key dimensions for identifying low-quality or harmful financial content. In consequence, high-quality finfluencers can be powerful allies in promoting better investment behaviors by addressing under-confidence, financial education gaps and risk tolerance (Heo et al. 2021). Notably, they could also contribute to overcoming the barriers that consumers encounter when obtaining financial advice (Moss et al. 2022). This is enabled by audience segmentation based on cognitive styles, enhancing content effectiveness and long-term financial decision-making and literacy (Estelami and Estelami 2024). Ultimately, finfluencers might also impact consumer behavior, supporting the adoption of new technologies through both community-building and endorsements (Nguyen et al. 2024). Our FinQ framework promotes transparency and indicates quality, which is essential for the selection of trustworthy finfluencers for all kinds of strategic partnerships. Thus, our work supports stakeholders to identify relevant high-quality finfluencers with which to engage in partnerships, thereby reducing the risk of reputational damages from finfluencing charlatans (Singh et al. 2020; Hudders et al. 2021). Further, such stakeholders save internal resources and reduce their risk exposure through guiding their selection strategy of relevant finfluencers (Saima and Khan 2021; Ren et al. 2023; Venciute et al. 2023). To further increase managerial usability, we developed two complementary tools based on the validated framework: (1) a FinQ scorecard (see Appendix C) that enables systematic benchmarking (FinQ-Score), and (2) a due diligence checklist (see Appendix E) that translates the validated KPIs into practical binary (yes or no) questions. These tools empower managers to conduct efficient, transparent, and criteria-based finfluencer assessments. In consequence, we see three main stake-holding corporate beneficiaries: First, our work guides sales and marketing managers of financial services businesses to extend their social media engagement and to select relevant high-quality finfluencers as trusted voices and brand ambassadors to especially target the digitally savvy younger generations (Anand and Mishra 2022; Mensah and Amenuvor 2022; Ho and Amin 2023; Chikweche et al. 2024; Lom et al. 2024; Nguyen et al. 2024). Second, our work also supports publicly listed companies in selecting finfluencers for extending their financial communication strategies targeted at attracting younger retail investors to diversify their shareholder basis (Binder-Tietz et al. 2024; Mölders et al. 2025). Finally, our work offers insights to financial planners and advisers, enabling them to gain a deeper understanding of finfluencers as competitors that present a challenge to their current business model (Holger 2020; Tkachenko 2022; Fava 2023; Reiter et al. 2023). In addition to corporate beneficiaries, we find that our research could also support managers of regulatory bodies and policymakers to refine current regulatory practices and consumer protection even though we acknowledge that the FinQ framework can only complement rethinking the current regulation of finfluencers (Aggarwal et al. 2022; Miller 2022; Pflücke 2022, 2023; Guan 2023, 2024; Hall 2023; Stefanou 2023; Espeute and Preece 2024). Our exploratory research is inherently limited due to the dynamic regulatory and technological environment, but also particularly regarding the variable understanding of quality itself. Thus, future research should further differentiate how the FinQ framework handles various finfluencer types, as quality may mean different things for licensed advisors versus unqualified promoters of high-risk assets (potential charlatans). In addition, the FinQ scorecard itself offers the potential to profile such different types (e.g., highly professional, lifestyle-oriented, and entertainment-driven). Future studies could leverage these profiles to map the distribution of finfluencer types across markets and platforms, tracking how they evolve over time. Further, our research is limited by involving an in-depth but small qualitative sample. Also, while mixed-method approaches enhance triangulation of data, they can lead to biases (Campbell et al. 2020; Creswell and Creswell 2023). In consequence, we encourage future studies to employ larger-scale quantitative designs to validate, refine, and potentially generalize our findings, for instance, by testing the FinQ-Score against the actual performance of investment recommendations from a broader set of scored finfluencers. Furthermore, reliance on historical data may not accurately reflect status quo. Thus, further research could aim at keeping objectivity and up-to-dateness (e.g., using AI-powered solutions or follower involvement) and extend the FinQ framework into a longitudinal tool, enabling repeated assessments to capture shifts in reputation and content quality over time. In addition, platform differences present a challenge to applicability and validity of our multiple-case study highlighting the need for cross-platform research. Lastly, we were unable to include all KPIs in the refined FinQ framework due to feasibility constraints, lack of public assessability or observability, and the exclusion of overly subjective or redundant indicators. Moreover, our engagement scoring may not fully capture distortions from bots or fake accounts. Future applications of the FinQ framework should integrate bot-audit tools and fraud detection to enhance robustness. Additionally, our FinQ framework is based on German and European Union law, which might necessitate adjustments of the Compliance dimension for its application to other markets and countries. In response, we call for the development of “generally accepted quality standards for finfluencers” as an extension to our FinQ framework. Finally, future studies could evaluate the FinQ framework’s effectiveness by linking it to actual success metrics of strategic partnerships with finfluencers, such as lead generation, return on investment (ROI), or changes in brand trust. While promising, this step requires longitudinal or other designs that go beyond the scope of our study. Nonetheless, we highlight it as a critical future direction to assess the long-term impact and strategic value of high-quality finfluencer partnerships. Ultimately, other researchers may use our framework as groundwork and provide novel further insights into leveraging high-quality finfluencers as a distinctive opportunity for the financial services industry to cultivate trust, engage with diverse audiences, and develop tailored marketing strategies that resonate with younger consumers in the age of social media. Returning to the introductory quote of the antihero Gordon Gekko, finfluencers currently lack accountability, and their mostly commission-based business model involves moral hazard and behavioral biases which not only causes quality uncertainty, but also potential consumer harm and financial losses. In response, we acknowledge the need for reassessing their current regulation to protect consumers (e.g., through setting minimum quality constraints). However, we see a risk of overregulating finfluencers, which could be not only costly from an economic perspective, but also may take away the opportunity to integrate finfluencers as potential allies in corporate and governmental strategic partnerships. In response, our FinQ framework is a complementary approach to tackle the current lack of accountability. In this context, our findings could accelerate professionalization by enabling informed decision making to eventually drive finfluencing charlatans out of the market. Further, our research builds on improving trustworthiness as a basis for encouraging strategic partnerships with high-quality finfluencers. We also find that regulators and governmental bodies should uphold a direct dialogue with high-quality finfluencers to integrate them into policymaking and strategies on promoting financial education, inclusion and literacy. We find that finfluencers may prove to be more than a short-term phenomenon or a further source of noise within the capital markets. Rather, they may represent a long-term game changer for the digital financial community and the financial services industry. Category Law or rules Requirement Unfair commercial practices § 5a (6) German Act against Unfair Competition (UWG) If finfluencers are compensated in return for posts, referrals, or the use of affiliate links, they must indicate this transparently. § 5 German Telemedia Act (TMG) As finfluencers usually follow commercial intentions because of their business model, they have an imprint obligation. § 22 (1) German Interstate Media Treaty (MStV) Finfluencers must make promotion clearly recognizable and clearly separate it from the other (editorial) content they produce. Financial advertising and capital market law § 34 German Trade Regulations (GewO) License must be acquired from the responsible Chamber of Commerce and Industry (IHK) for any financial advisory activities. § 32 (1) of the German Banking Act (KWG) Advanced licenses need to be acquired for actively trading or managing customer assets or securities. § 20 German Market Abuse Regulation (MMVO) Finfluencers must ensure objectivity and disclose their own interests or conflicts of interest related to financial tips they share. § 12 German Asset Investment Act (VermAnlG) When promoting high-risk financial products, finfluencers may need to disclose a warning in accordance with § 12 VermAnlG. Intellectual property rights German Law on Copyrights and Related Rights) Finfluencers must comply with copyright rules. Data protection German General Data Protection Regulation (DSGVO/GDPR) Finfluencers must comply with data protections rules. Others Private terms of services of social media platforms Finfluencers must fulfill terms of services of corresponding platforms. Topic Question (1) Background and context (a) Please briefly introduce yourself and, if applicable, tell us about your role and experience in dealing with finfluencers. (b) How do you define a finfluencer? (2) Quality indicators (a) What indicators characterize a high-quality finfluencer for you? Optional follow-up question: On the other hand, what indicators do you think characterize a low-quality finfluencer? (b) In your opinion, to what extent do quality indicators of finfluencers differ from those of other influencer niches (e.g., beauty, fashion, fitness, etc.)? (c) Which indicators would you use to assess the quality of finfluencers? (3) Challenges and controversies (a) What challenges and problems do you see in the development of a finfluencer quality rating? Optional follow-up question: Regarding the diversity of financial topics and the different target groups? (b) What role do conflicts of interest and transparency issues play in the quality assessment of finfluencers? (c) Are there ethical or legal aspects that should be taken into consideration when assessing the quality of a finfluencer? (4) Practicability and feasibility (a) What do you think about the feasibility of a finfluencer quality assessment in the real world? Optional follow-up question: Which stakeholders could benefit from it? (b) Which approaches, information sources or methods do you think are most suitable for measuring the quality of finfluencers? (5) Outlook and regulation (a) How do you see the future of finfluencers and their impact on the financial world of tomorrow, especially also in the context of financial communication? (b) What roles do regulation and control play? Optional follow-up questions: What exactly should be regulated and controlled? Who should be responsible for this? Independent sub-score of the dimension Engagement (QD5) [15% Weight] Σ 0.73 0.8 0.8 0.8 0.47 FinQ-Score Weighted quality score Σ 0.8 0.92 0.81 N/A 0.77 Note. F = Filter (all-or-nothing principle); M = Likert-type scale of 0 to 5 (manual analyst assessment); D = Dichotomous scale of 0 or 5 (all-or-nothing principle); B = Likert-type scale of 0 to 5 (peer benchmark). The FinQ-Score is based on five quality dimensions. Compliance acts as a gatekeeper: critical breaches exclude a finfluencer from scoring. The remaining four dimensions are weighed unequally: Content and Communication and Credibility are prioritized as most relevant (35%), while Profile Structure and Engagement are underweighted (15%) due to limited finfluencer specificity, risk of bias from popularity, and susceptibility to fake engagement. Despite a small sample (n = 5), the results show strong quality and diversity, illustrating the practical value of the FinQ framework for future applications. Title: Interactive Brokers: Nvidia Is Most Heavily Bought Stock Post-Earnings
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/03/04/interactive-brokers-nvidia-is-most-heavily-bought-stock-post-earnings/
Time Published: 2026-03-04T15:29:47Z
Full Content:
Investing Interactive Brokers (IBKR) customers heavily bought Nvidia (NVDA), down 6.64% to $180.05 despite reporting $68.13B revenue (up 73.2%). Interactive Brokers customers bought Nvidia’s post-earnings dip, treating the disconnect between record revenue growth and the subsequent stock decline as a buying opportunity. Sending You to Google News in 3 © pestoverde / Flickr When Interactive Brokers (NASDAQ:IBKR) talks about what its customers are actually doing with their money, it’s worth listening. These aren’t passive index fund holders. They’re active, globally diversified traders who move fast and think technically. And right now, they’ve been piling into one name above all others. “Nvidia has been the name with us particularly after earnings. So it’s been basically by far the most active name and the most heavily bought name in trading through yesterday,” an Interactive Brokers representative said recently. “So that’s pretty much a post-earnings event.” The timing makes sense. Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA | NVDA Price Prediction) reported a genuinely stunning quarter on February 25, 2026. Revenue came in at $68.13 billion, up 73.2% year-over-year, beating estimates by nearly 3%. Adjusted EPS hit $1.62, topping the consensus of $1.52. Data Center revenue alone reached $62.31 billion, up 75%. And forward guidance pointed to $78 billion in Q1 FY2027 revenue. Yet the stock sold off afterward. NVDA is down 6.64% over the past week and sits at $180.05 as of March 3. That disconnect between blockbuster results and price action is exactly what IBKR customers treated as an opportunity. Jensen Huang framed the big picture on the earnings call: “Computing demand is growing exponentially — the agentic AI inflection point has arrived. Grace Blackwell with NVLink is the king of inference today — delivering an order-of-magnitude lower cost per token — and Vera Rubin will extend that leadership even further.” That’s a roadmap, not a victory lap. IBKR’s customers appear to be betting on the roadmap. The broader trading behavior at IBKR tells an equally interesting story about market psychology right now. The representative noted that customers were buying dips late last week, then selling into the subsequent rally — adding that few had “up day with the Straits of Hormuz closed on their bingo card.” “In general, what we’re seeing here is the buy-the-dip mentality remains very strong. It’s also very technically driven. The buyers came in right around the 100-day moving average. And the buying ended pretty much right at the 50-day moving average.” The S&P held near late December lows around 5,715. The VIX, now at 21.44 and in elevated uncertainty territory, never entered backwardation — meaning short-end volatility futures never traded above long-end futures, which would signal a real shortage of protection and deeper panic. That’s a constructive signal, even if the market feels uneasy. Small caps are up 5.31% year-to-date per IWM, while the broader S&P is essentially flat at -0.23% YTD. For now, IBKR’s customers are telling us exactly what they think: Nvidia’s post-earnings pullback has drawn significant buying activity from their active trader base. Title: The AI Jobs Apocalypse Is Starting to Feel Real
URL: http://futurism.com/artificial-intelligence/ai-jobs-apocalypse-feeling-real
Time Published: 2026-03-04T14:02:35Z
Full Content:
By Frank Landymore Published Mar 4, 2026 9:02 AM EST Sign up to see the future, today Can’t-miss innovations from the bleeding edge of science and tech It’s difficult to say how many jobs have been lost to AI, or will be lost in the future. But in tech and financial circles, anxieties over an AI jobs apocalypse are running higher than ever. The other week, it was a viral paper from Citrini Research imagining a dire near future in which vast portions of the workforce are outmoded by AI that had Wall Street quivering in its boots. Weeks before that, Anthropic’s new Claude Cowork AI agent sparked a mass stock selloff over fears that it could automate tasks like legal work, wiping out billions of dollars. Tech leaders have warned of AI’s potential to disrupt the job market for years, but it’s only in recent months that the atmosphere has felt so high-strung. Additional fuel was thrown onto the fire when, last week, Block CEO Jack Dorsey announced that his fintech firm, formerly known as Square, was firing 4,000 employees, or nearly half its workforce. Though Dorsey attributed the layoffs to pandemic overhiring, he also enthused that “intelligence tools” were creating a “new way of working” with smaller teams. This sounded alarm bells across the industry, per a roundup of reactions by The Wall Street Journal. “Square is just the beginning,” former Meta and Salesforce executive Clara Shih wrote on X, responding to a lengthy post claiming that the Block layoffs are “the first AI cut.” “A lot of the jobs that we’ve thrown human beings at the last 20 or 30 years, you won’t need as many human beings doing those same jobs,” Amazon CEO Andy Jassy said in a CNBC interview when asked about Dorsey’s cuts flagged by the WSJ. The fears are warranted, but likely overstated at the present moment. There’s no denying that firms are laying off droves of employees, and there’s also no denying that many of them are openly enthusiastic about AI. One report found that AI was cited in the announcements of more than 54,000 layoffs last year. Those employers include Amazon, which is in the midst of culling 14,000 employees after boasting of the “efficiency gains” from deploying AI across the company. But are autonomous systems actually replacing those jobs? Experts caution that executives may simply be using AI as an excuse to justify cuts that were driven by purely financial logic, a phenomenon that’s being dubbed “AI-washing.” Marcelo P. Lima, founder and managing partner at Heller House, disputed alarmists claims that Dorsey’s cuts at Block were AI driven, calling them the “new Citrini fake narrative.” “Everyone will assume Jack Dorsey… is doing this because of AI,” he wrote on X. “He’s not.” The reality, Lima explained, was that the company was bloated in the first place. Like many tech companies during the pandemic, it had hired thousands of new employees, ballooning its workforce from 3,900 in 2019 to 12,500 in 2022. There’s also little evidence that AI systems are currently capable of taking over human roles in any robust sense. Numerous studies have found that even the most capable AI agents fail at common white collar tasks, while providing little economic benefit: a widely cited MIT study found that 95 percent of companies that integrated AI saw no meaningful increase in revenue. An additional body of research has illustrated how AI can negatively impact human employees, dragging down efficiency and even intensifying work. Still, the fear of something is often more damaging than the thing itself, and if the market is constantly rattled by AI’s still vague potential to disrupt if not upend the job market, it could cause economic uncertainty to fester for years to come. More on AI: Harvard Professor Says AI Users Are Losing Cognitive Abilities I’m a tech and science correspondent for Futurism, where I’m particularly interested in astrophysics, the business and ethics of artificial intelligence and automation, and the environment. By Frank Landymore By Frank Landymore By Joe Wilkins By Joe Wilkins By Joe Wilkins By Victor Tangermann By Joe Wilkins By Frank Landymore By Victor Tangermann By Frank Landymore By Joe Wilkins By Sharon Adarlo By Victor Tangermann By Frank Landymore By Victor Tangermann By Frank Landymore By Rae Witte By Frank Landymore By Joe Wilkins By Joe Wilkins By Victor Tangermann Disclaimer(s) Articles may contain affiliate links which enable us to share in the revenue of any purchases made. Title: Nvidia invests $4 billion into photonics firms in a bid to bolster data center interconnect supply chains — Lumentum and Coherent investment to fund U.S. R&D and manufacturing facilities, supports capacity rights and future access
URL: https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/nvidia-invests-usd4-billion-into-photonics-firms-in-a-bid-to-bolster-data-center-interconnect-supply-chains-lumentum-and-coherent-investment-to-fund-u-s-r-and-d-and-manufacturing-facilities-supports-capacity-rights-and-future-access
Time Published: 2026-03-04T14:01:18Z
Full Content:
Into the photonic future. When you purchase through links on our site, we may earn an affiliate commission. Here’s how it works. Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox. You are now subscribed Your newsletter sign-up was successful Nvidia has pledged to invest a combined $4 billion in two photonic manufacturing firms, Lumentum and Coherent. As Reuters reports, this continues Nvidia's plans to expend capital investment with its expansive cash reserves to help build the AI ecosystem and improve the output of its models. The quiet part is that it also helps keep Nvidia on the cutting edge. Both companies develop optical and semiconductor technologies, enabling future networking hardware and AI chip designs that utilize light to transfer data, rather than electricity. With Nvidia looking to continue providing the digital shovels in this AI gold rush, leveraging forward-looking technologies like photonics may be one way it maintains its edge in the range of hardware spaces it now occupies in the global AI buildout. “AI has reinvented computing and is driving the largest computing infrastructure buildout in history,” said Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang. “Together with Lumentum, NVIDIA is advancing the world’s most sophisticated silicon photonics to build the next generation of gigawatt-scale AI factories.” Jim Anderson, CEO of Coherent, spoke on their deal, stating: “This strategic relationship underscores Coherent’s role as a key enabler of next-generation AI data center infrastructure. We are proud to expand our 20-year relationship with NVIDIA by increasing their access to include multiple product families to help them build the AI data centers of the future.” Nvidia has been working with both of these companies for some time on its own networking hardware and to develop a strong supply chain for these key components. These new deals only enhance that cooperation. As with most of the deals within and without the AI industry over the last year, Nvidia's investment in these firms is a mixture of hard currency, future purchase orders, and secured access to key technologies. With Lumentum, Nvidia's $2 billion investment will support its R&D and manufacturing expansion in the U.S. with a new fabrication facility. It also includes a "multibillion purchase commitment" from Nvidia, and "future capacity access rights for advanced laser components." The Coherent deal reads almost identically. There, Nvidia and Coherant talk up the "multiyear strategic agreement" between the two companies, which includes "an Nvidia multibillion-dollar purchase commitment and future access and capacity rights for advanced laser and optical networking products." In addition, Nvidia will also invest $2 billion to support R&D and fab. expansion for its U.S.-based manufacturing facilities. Considering the number of bottlenecks that have appeared since the memory supply shortage began in 2025, from glass cloth to PCB drill bits, Nvidia's investment in expanding photonics development and fabrication in America may be a way to get ahead of future supply constraints, too. With the similarities between these two investment vehicles for Nvidia, and the near identical nature of the deals and subsequent press releases, it very much feels like Nvidia is hedging its bets. Arguably, that's what Nvidia has been doing since the advent of this major AI revolution. Nvidia is the one major company making money hand over fist in this new era, and instead of hoarding it, or using it exclusively for stock buybacks, or corporate bonuses, it's been reinvesting in the AI industry that put a rocket up its balance sheets and stock price. It invested in Coreweave, OpenAI, Oracle, Intel, Synopsys, and Nokia, all of which are heavily involved in the AI industry or are adjacent to it. It's keenly aware that even as the king of the AI pile, it needs those other economic and industry pillars to keep the whole house of cards standing. If Nvidia were bold enough, it could try to buy out Lumentum and Coherent entirely. Their company valuations are a fraction of Nvidia's profits from even the past quarter. But it didn't. It secured capacity, but not exclusive rights. Nvidia isn't looking to eat the AI industry and supply chain, but bolster its output. As much as Nvidia is the clear market leader when it comes to AI hardware, it is far from the only player in the game. China has used Nvidia's absence to heavily invest and fuel its domestic inference chip industry. AMD recently signed a $60 billion deal with Meta to power its next-generation AI-powering data centers, and there are small startup companies looking to build out ASIC chips that could prove far more powerful for future AI workloads when fully developed. Marvell's Celestial AI acquisition highlights that other companies see optical technologies as a key future technology for next-generation networking hardware. Meanwhile, the Bill Gates-backed Neurophos is developing AI chips that use photonics to accelerate even beyond what the best GPUs can do. Nvidia made itself the king of the AI hill by having the best hardware at the right time, and as far as training goes, that doesn't seem likely to change any time soon. But if Nvidia doesn't keep developing its hardware, other contenders would emerge. And the competition is ramping up in the inference space, leaving no guarantee that Nvidia will retain its premier position forever. Perhaps getting ahead of the pack when it comes to photonics will be one way it can maintain its lead. But even if it doesn't, $4 billion is a drop in the bucket for a company that made close to 20 times that in the past three months alone. Jon Martindale is a contributing writer for Tom's Hardware. For the past 20 years, he's been writing about PC components, emerging technologies, and the latest software advances. His deep and broad journalistic experience gives him unique insights into the most exciting technology trends of today and tomorrow. Tom's Hardware is part of Future US Inc, an international media group and leading digital publisher. Visit our corporate site. © Future US, Inc. Full 7th Floor, 130 West 42nd Street, New York, NY 10036.

Title: Prediction: Costco Will Surge After March 5th
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/03/04/prediction-costco-will-surge-after-march-5th/
Time Published: 2026-03-04T13:47:58Z
Full Content:
Investing Costco (COST) is up 17.03% YTD to $1,007.77, with membership fee income of $1.329B (up 14.0%), e-commerce sales up 20.5%, an 89.7% renewal rate, and a 12-month price target of $1,052.94. The September 2024 membership fee increase provides recurring revenue at near-100% margins independent of consumer behavior, creating a structural earnings tailwind as e-commerce growth accelerates. Sending You to Google News in 3 © 2024 Getty Images / Getty Images News via Getty Images Costco shares have climbed 17% year-to-date, reaching $1,007.77 to close out March 3rd, a stark contrast to the S&P 500, which is essentially flat at -0.24% YTD. Yet despite that outperformance, broader sentiment around the stock remains cautious, with tariff uncertainty and rising labor costs weighing on investor confidence in the consumer defensive space. The underlying business, however, continues to compound quietly and consistently. Membership renewal rates are holding firm, e-commerce is accelerating, and cash generation is strengthening. With earnings on March 5, 2026 approaching, the setup is in place for Costco to reset the narrative. The most important thing to understand about Costco’s earnings model is that a significant portion of its profit is essentially pre-collected. Membership fee income hit $1.329 billion in Q1 FY2026, up 14.0% year-over-year, and that revenue stream carries near-100% margins. With 81.4 million paid memberships and a worldwide renewal rate of 89.7%, the fee base is both large and sticky. The September 2024 membership fee increase continues to flow through the income statement, providing a structural tailwind that doesn’t depend on consumer spending behavior. Beyond membership, Costco’s e-commerce channel is delivering outsized growth. E-commerce comparable sales rose 20.5% in Q1 FY2026, with site traffic up 24% and average order value up 13%. That momentum has been consistent across recent quarters — digital comp sales have ranged from 13.6% to 20.9% over the past four quarters — and shows no sign of plateauing. The earnings beat track record reinforces the bull case. Costco has beaten EPS estimates in eight consecutive quarters, with surprise margins ranging from 0.86% to 6.32%. Prediction markets are pricing in continued outperformance: Polymarket currently shows a 92.5% probability that Costco beats quarterly earnings, with the market expiring on March 5. Full-year analyst estimates reflect sustained growth, with quarterly earnings growth running at 11.4% year-over-year — well above the broader market average. That combination of recurring revenue visibility, digital acceleration, and a consistent beat pattern positions Costco well heading into Thursday’s print. Costco trades at a forward P/E of 49.75x — a meaningful premium to the broader market. That premium is justified by the earnings growth profile: quarterly earnings growth of 11.4% year-over-year, underpinned by a membership model that generates predictable, high-margin fee income regardless of macroeconomic conditions. The business also carries a return on equity of 30.3%, which reflects the capital efficiency of the warehouse model at scale. Analyst consensus supports the upside case. The 12-month consensus price target stands at $1,052.94, implying roughly 4.5% upside from current levels. Of the 37 analysts covering the stock, 23 carry Buy ratings versus 12 Holds and just 2 Sells. The stock is currently trading below its 52-week high of $1,062.65, meaning it hasn’t fully recovered from last year’s pullback even as the business has continued to strengthen. With membership income compounding, e-commerce accelerating, and a near-unbroken streak of earnings beats, Thursday’s results represent the most immediate catalyst for the stock to close that gap. Any word on a Costco special dividend will immediately send the stock higher. Costco has a history of returning excess cash to shareholders through large special dividends, typically every few years when its balance sheet builds significant surplus cash. The company paid $7 per share in 2012, $5 in 2015, $7 in 2017, $10 in 2020, and most recently $15 per share in late 2023, its largest payout ever. These one-time distributions have become a recurring feature of Costco’s capital return strategy and are closely watched by investors, as announcements have historically served as a positive catalyst for the stock. Title: Why US stock futures suddenly surged today: Dow, S&P 500 and Nasdaq turn green — Is Wall Street returning to its growth path now?
URL: https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/international/us/why-us-stock-futures-suddenly-surged-today-dow-futures-sp-500-and-nasdaq-turn-green-is-wall-street-returning-to-its-growth-path-now/articleshow/129025557.cms
Time Published: 2026-03-04T12:09:27Z
Full Content:
US stock futures turned green early Wednesday. Dow futures jumped to 48,598. S&P 500 futures climbed to 6,837. Nasdaq futures rose to 24,824. The rebound followed a sharp sell-off on Wall Street a day earlier. Markets reacted after reports said Iran quietly approached the US for talks to ease the conflict with Israel. That signal cooled oil fears. Brent crude hovered near $82 while WTI stayed around $75. Lower panic brought buyers back into equities. Investors now watch ADP payroll data and the US jobs report closely. Strong economic signals could push Dow, S&P 500, and Nasdaq further up this week. Title: Prediction: Nvidia (NVDA) Will Be Worth More Than Alphabet, Apple, Amazon, Tesla, Meta, and Microsoft Combined by 2030
URL: https://consent.yahoo.com/v2/collectConsent?sessionId=1_cc-session_b5891782-8511-492b-91d8-a2f4c8f8a40e
